judgments.comparative_verdict: Report 2 is substantially better: it is grounded in the actual juniper repo and correctly identifies the ROTA-214 commit and owner-exemption mechanism, whereas Report 1 describes a mismatched "cedar" codebase, wrongly attributes the mechanism partly to superuser, and gets the exploit-timeline commit wrong.

report_1.evidence: which is both the schema/table owner (it runs the migrations via scripts/migrate.sh) and the cluster superuser (POSTGRES_USER: cedar_owner in docker-compose.yml; the postgres image makes POSTGRES_USER a superuser). In Postgres, a superuser bypasses Row-Level Security unconditionally, and a table owner also bypasses it unless the table has FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY.

report_2.evidence: a table's owner is exempt from RLS policies when only ENABLE is set — the policy is simply not applied to the owner role. FORCE is what makes the policy apply to the owner as well. The deployment connects as juniper_owner (docker-compose.yml:5, .env.example:1), which is the role that created/owns the appointments table

report_1.evidence: .env/.env.example were created pointing at cedar_owner in commit a24f68f (CED-1 ... 2026-05-04) ... the runtime has never used a non-bypassing role.

report_2.evidence: Commit b2a23d2 — "ROTA-214 run migrations from the api container on boot" ... The diff changes the runtime DATABASE_URL in both docker-compose.yml and .env.example (and infra/main.tf) from juniper_app:apppw to juniper_owner:ownerpw.

report_1.evidence: change the runtime DATABASE_URL to the least-privilege cedar_app role ... Force RLS at the DB layer: add a migration with ALTER TABLE clients FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; and ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

report_2.evidence: Revert the runtime DATABASE_URL back to the low-privilege juniper_app role ... Add defense-in-depth in the migration: add ALTER TABLE appointments FORCE ROW LEVEL SECURITY; in a new migration

report_1.evidence: Application-level filter (belt and suspenders): add WHERE tenant_id = :tenant_id to queries in main.py so a future RLS misconfiguration can't silently leak.
Boot-time guard: in backend/app/db.py (or a FastAPI startup hook in main.py), assert the connected role is not superuser and cannot bypass RLS ... and refuse to start otherwise

report_2.evidence: add a startup assertion or CI check that the runtime DATABASE_URL role is not the owner of RLS-protected tables ... and refuse to boot if a protected table is owned by the connecting role, or if any tenant table has RLS enabled but not forced
